---
title: "Fitting delay distributions with estimate_dist()"
output:
  rmarkdown::html_vignette:
    toc: true
    number_sections: true
bibliography: library.bib
csl: https://raw.githubusercontent.com/citation-style-language/styles/master/apa-numeric-superscript-brackets.csl
vignette: >
  %\VignetteIndexEntry{Fitting delay distributions with estimate_dist()}
  %\VignetteEngine{knitr::rmarkdown}
  %\VignetteEncoding{UTF-8}
---



# Introduction

`estimate_dist()` fits delay distributions from linelist data while accounting for interval censoring and right truncation.
These biases arise routinely in epidemiological data where event times are recorded at daily resolution and recent observations are missing due to reporting delays.
The function uses a Stan model that vendors likelihood functions from the [primarycensored](https://primarycensored.epinowcast.org/) package.
If you use `estimate_dist()`, please cite `primarycensored` in addition to `EpiNow2`.

The result is an `<estimate_dist>` object (inheriting from `<epinowfit>`) that gives access to the Stan fit for diagnostics.
Use `get_parameters()` to extract the fitted `<dist_spec>`, ready for use in other _EpiNow2_ functions such as `estimate_infections()`.

For more flexible delay distribution modelling, see the [epidist](https://epidist.epinowcast.org/) package.

# Set up


``` r
library(EpiNow2) # nolint: unused_import_linter.
library(primarycensored)
#> Warning: package 'primarycensored' was built under R version 4.5.2
options(mc.cores = 2)
```

# Simulating censored delay data

We simulate 200 observations from a lognormal delay distribution with known parameters.
`rprimarycensored()` generates delays subject to daily censoring and right truncation at `D = 30` days.


``` r
set.seed(42)
n <- 200
D <- 30

true_meanlog <- 1.5
true_sdlog <- 0.75

pdate_lwr <- as.Date("2023-01-01") +
  sample(0:13, n, replace = TRUE)

delays <- rprimarycensored(
  n = n,
  rdist = rlnorm,
  meanlog = true_meanlog,
  sdlog = true_sdlog,
  pwindow = 1,
  D = D
)

linelist <- data.frame(
  pdate_lwr = pdate_lwr,
  sdate_lwr = pdate_lwr + delays,
  obs_date = pdate_lwr + D
)

head(linelist)
#>    pdate_lwr  sdate_lwr   obs_date
#> 1 2023-01-01 2023-01-03 2023-01-31
#> 2 2023-01-05 2023-01-11 2023-02-04
#> 3 2023-01-01 2023-01-04 2023-01-31
#> 4 2023-01-09 2023-01-19 2023-02-08
#> 5 2023-01-10 2023-01-10 2023-02-09
#> 6 2023-01-04 2023-01-19 2023-02-03
```

The data frame has three columns:

- `pdate_lwr`: lower bound of the primary event date (e.g. date of infection).
- `sdate_lwr`: lower bound of the secondary event date (e.g. date of symptom onset).
- `obs_date`: the date at which the data were extracted, determining the right truncation point for each observation.

Upper bounds for the primary and secondary event windows default to one day after the lower bounds (daily censoring).

# Fitting the model

We pass the linelist directly to `estimate_dist()`.
Internally the function converts dates to numeric intervals, aggregates identical delay–censoring combinations, and fits the model via MCMC.


``` r
result <- estimate_dist(
  linelist,
  dist = "lognormal"
)
params <- get_parameters(result)
params$delay
#> - lognormal distribution (max: 29):
#>   meanlog:
#>     - normal distribution:
#>       mean:
#>         1.4
#>       sd:
#>         0.061
#>   sdlog:
#>     - normal distribution:
#>       mean:
#>         0.81
#>       sd:
#>         0.053
```

# Checking parameter recovery

We compare the fitted posterior means to the true values.


``` r
delay <- params$delay
fitted_meanlog <- delay$parameters$meanlog$parameters$mean
fitted_sdlog <- delay$parameters$sdlog$parameters$mean

data.frame(
  parameter = c("meanlog", "sdlog"),
  true = c(true_meanlog, true_sdlog),
  fitted = round(c(fitted_meanlog, fitted_sdlog), 2)
)
#>   parameter true fitted
#> 1   meanlog 1.50   1.44
#> 2     sdlog 0.75   0.81
```

# What if I don't have dates?

If you have numeric delay values without dates, there are two options.

You can use [primarycensored](https://primarycensored.epinowcast.org/) directly, which works with numeric delays and provides the same underlying model.

Alternatively, you can construct a date-based data frame from numeric delays using dummy dates:


``` r
origin <- as.Date("2020-01-01")
linelist <- data.frame(
  pdate_lwr = origin,
  sdate_lwr = origin + delays,
  obs_date = origin + D
)
```

This lets you use `estimate_dist()` as shown above.
